title: Simon Hamra
text:
A job market in SQL
I turned one flat Lightcast file into a small data warehouse, then answered four real questions about pay and hiring in Spark SQL.



Problem


A single wide table is hard to query and full of repeated values. I wanted clean, joinable tables and a few clear answers about pay and hiring.




Constraints


The same large Lightcast file, so I built and queried everything in Spark SQL instead of loading it into memory.




Approach


I split the data into dimension tables for companies, industries and locations, each with its own surrogate key, then joined them back into one fact table of postings. That is a star schema. On top of it I wrote four queries: median pay by specialized occupation inside the Technology industry (NAICS 518210), the top five remote employers in California, month-by-month California posting volume, and average pay across twelve major US metros.




Tradeoff


I could have kept one flat table and queried it directly, which is faster to set up. I chose the star schema because the joins stay clean, the dimension tables are reusable, and it is closer to how a real data warehouse works.




Result


1 star schema, 4 queries. Pay is highest in expensive metros like San Francisco and Seattle, remote hiring concentrates in a few large employers, and California posting volume dips over the summer then recovers toward the end of the year.




PySparkSpark SQLStar schemaJoinsseabornmatplotlib


Repository & notebook

title: Simon Hamra
text:
Salary insights at scale
I took a 700 MB Lightcast file, cleaned it in Spark, and turned it into clear pictures of who gets paid what.



Problem


The file holds tens of thousands of job postings, but it is messy. Salary, experience and education all load as text, and most rows do not even list a salary. I needed clean, honest numbers I could chart.




Constraints


The raw file is close to 700 MB, too big to open in pandas on a laptop. So all the heavy work, loading, casting, medians and grouping, had to stay in Spark. I only pulled small, already-aggregated tables into pandas right before drawing each chart.




Approach


I cast the salary and experience columns to numbers, computed medians in Spark, and filled missing salaries with a careful midpoint rule, keeping a separate copy of the real observed salary so a reported median never uses a filled value. Then I built five visuals on a custom Plotly theme: a salary-floor box plot by industry and employment type, a median-pay bubble chart by occupation, experience-versus-pay scatters split by education and by remote type, and an overlaid salary histogram.




Tradeoff


I had to choose between dropping rows with no salary or filling them. Dropping throws away most of the data; filling keeps it but can hide the truth. My answer was to do both: fill for completeness, but always use the observed salary for any real median.




Result


72,498 postings cleaned and charted. Full-time roles pay more, remote and hybrid roles lean higher, and the best-paid occupation reaches about $158k while the most common roles sit near $95k to $126k.




PySparkpandasPlotlySeabornQuarto


Repository & notebook

title: Simon Hamra
text:
Predicting salary, and a trap
I trained three models to predict salary, and hit a classic trap that stops a model from giving any statistics.



Problem


I wanted to predict salary from experience, education, posting duration and how the role is delivered, and also explain which feature matters most.




Constraints


After I required a real salary plus every feature present, only about 2,000 rows were left out of the roughly 30,000 that had a salary. Small, but every value is real and nothing is filled in.




Approach


I built a Spark ML pipeline with indexing, one-hot encoding and a feature vector, then fit a generalized linear model, a polynomial model with a squared experience term, and a random forest with 200 trees and depth 8. The data was split 80 / 20 with a fixed seed, and I compared the models on RMSE, R², AIC and BIC.




The trap


Spark gave me coefficients but refused the standard errors, so no p-values and no confidence intervals. The cause was multicollinearity: the minimum and maximum experience columns were identical in every row, correlation exactly 1.0. I dropped one column and the statistics came back.




Result


Each extra year of experience is worth about $8,000, and that effect is strong. The random forest was the most accurate model, but the linear model explains the story best. Experience carries about 57% of the importance, far ahead of education and industry.




Spark MLRandom forestGLRPolynomialStats

text: Business and marketing first, data and ML now

title: Simon Hamra
section: My path is not a straight line
text:
My path is not a straight line
I started with a bachelor in management at Rennes School of Business, then a master in marketing at HULT. For a while I thought my career would be in marketing, and my two internships were in that world. One was in e-commerce in France, the other was in London handling the social media and public image of DJs.
But on almost every project, the part I enjoyed the most was the data. Looking at the numbers, finding a pattern, and explaining what it means in simple words. So I decided to stop doing it on the side and study analytics for real.
At Boston University, my analytics coursework is where I got hands-on with data at scale. In one course I worked through a series of projects on a large Lightcast job posting dataset: using PySpark to handle a file too big for a normal laptop, cleaning it, modeling it in SQL, and building charts and machine learning models on top. Those projects are what you see in this portfolio. On my own time I keep learning machine learning step by step, from simple regression up to neural networks and transformers.
What I want next is a first real experience in machine learning. It is the field I grew to love, and I learn the fastest when I work on a real problem with real data.

title: Simon Hamra
section: Three assignments, one dataset, real numbers
text:
Three assignments, one dataset, real numbers
These are the three graded projects from my AD688 analytics course, each on the same Lightcast job postings data. Every one shows a different skill, and I kept the heavy work in Spark and only pulled small tables out to draw the charts.
